title: Quilltap Custom Tool
description: Schema for a `Tools/*.tool.json` custom-tool (pseudo-tool) definition — a named action with parameters, a random roll, and an ordered table of outcomes. Place the file in a `Tools/` folder at the root of any document store; the tool's identity is its `name` field, not the filename. One tool per file. Kept in sync by hand with the Zod schema in lib/pascal/custom-tool.types.ts, which is the runtime source of truth.

$schema.description: Editor hint pointing at this schema. Ignored at runtime.

name.description: The tool's identity — what the model calls and what shadowing resolves on. Lowercase, starts with a letter, 1–64 characters. The filename is not significant.

title.description: How the tool is named to a human — in Pascal's announcement, in the composer popup, and in the roster list. Omit it and the `name` is title-cased instead ('scan_hawking_radiation' becomes 'Scan Hawking Radiation'), which is often enough. The model never sees this: it calls the tool by `name`.

description.description: What the tool does IN THE FICTION. This is how the model decides when to reach for it and how the UI labels it — write 'Attempt to pick the lock,' not 'rolls 0–1 against thresholds.'

disabled.description: true suppresses a tool of this name at this tier and every tier below it. Use to switch off an inherited tool for one character or project.

revealOdds.description: When false, the model sees only name/description/parameters — never the roll spec or outcome table. NOTE: this hides the odds from the tool roster only. The .tool.json file is still an ordinary document; a character with character_read on the store can read it with doc_* tools. For genuinely secret odds, put the file in a store the character cannot read.

defaultVisibility.description: Default visibility of the result. 'whisper' hides the outcome from other participants (a GM-style hidden roll); the human operator always sees it.

parameters.description: Optional inputs, keyed by parameter name (same identifier rules as `name`). At most 8.

default.description: Required. Every parameter needs a default so a zero-argument run always works. Must match the declared type, or be a { "$state": "path", "fallback": ... } reference whose fallback matches the declared type.

description.description: What this parameter means, in the fiction.

min.description: Numeric types only. Run-time values are clamped up to this.

max.description: Numeric types only. Run-time values are clamped down to this.

roll.description: How chance is drawn. Either a numeric-range object (Form A) or a dice-notation string (Form B). Omit for a plain uniform 0–1 roll.

oneOf.description: Form B — dice notation like "3d6+2", "1d20", "2d10-1". Rolled by Quilltap's shared dice roller (2–1000 sides, 1–100 dice, modifier within ±1000). The raw value is the dice total, and {{dice}} renders the breakdown. Dice notation does not accept $param references in v1.

Form A — a uniform float in [min, max), then transformed: value = raw * multiplier + offset, rounded last if `round` is set. Each numeric field accepts a literal number, a { "$param": "name" } reference to a declared numeric parameter, or a { "$state": "path", "fallback": <number> } reference.

multiplier.description: Raw value is multiplied by this first. Default 1.

round.description: Round the final value to a whole number. Applied last. Default false.

llm.description: Ask an LLM for a generated result. When present, every run renders `prompt` and poses it to the instance's cheap utility model AFTER the roll and BEFORE the outcome table. The result is a pair { ok, output }: on success, output is the model's trimmed answer; on any failure (provider error, timeout, empty answer, no model configured), ok is false and output is this block's errorMessage — the author's words, never the provider's. Either way, outcomes may test the pair via the `llm` subject and messages may render it via {{llm}}, so a failed consult is an outcome the table deals with rather than an error: the run itself never fails because the model went quiet.

prompt.description: What to ask, rendered with the same placeholders an outcome message takes — {{value}}, {{roll}}, {{dice}}, {{params.name}}, {{metadata.key}}, {{state.path}} — but not {{llm}} itself. Ask for the answer shape you intend to test: a bare word, a number, a sentence.

errorMessage.description: The consult's output when the call fails, in the author's own words. The technical reason goes to the roll record and the logs, never into the fiction.

maxOutput.description: Cap on the answer, in characters. Default 8000. Set it low for a verdict, high for a consult whose answer IS the deliverable — the call's token budget scales with it. Applies to the model's answer only, never to errorMessage.

outcomes.description: Ordered outcome table; the first match wins. The LAST entry must be a `true` catch-all so every roll lands somewhere, and no earlier entry may be `true` (everything below it would be unreachable). At most 32.

when.description: The test. Either the literal `true` (catch-all) or an object naming one or more subjects, ALL of which must hold. Bare comparators test the final value — `>= 0.30 && <= 0.60` is { "gte": 0.30, "lte": 0.60 }. `roll` tests the raw pre-transform draw, `params` tests what the caller supplied, so "value > 1 and scale > 12" is { "gt": 1, "params": { "scale": { "gt": 12 } } }, `metadata` tests the invoking character's own metadata.json fact sheet, and `llm` tests the LLM consult's answer (or its success, via `ok`). There is no OR and no nesting: ordered first-match-wins outcomes make OR unnecessary, and this keeps the evaluator free of any expression grammar.

gte.description: Value is greater than or equal to.

lte.description: Value is less than or equal to.

roll.description: Test the RAW pre-transform draw rather than the final value. Useful when multiplier/offset/round have moved the value away from what was actually rolled.

params.description: Test the resolved parameters, keyed by parameter name. Each must name a declared parameter. Ordering comparators (gt/gte/lt/lte) require a numeric parameter; eq/neq may address a string or boolean one; contains/ncontains search a string parameter for a substring.

metadata.description: Test the INVOKING CHARACTER's metadata.json fact sheet, keyed by metadata key — { "metadata": { "hasAnsibleAccess": { "eq": true } } }. Keys are the user's own, so any non-empty string is allowed and nothing about them can be validated when the file loads. At run time a key the character does not have, or one holding a list/object/null, or one whose type the comparator cannot sustain (ordering a string), simply does not match: the row is passed over and the table falls through to its catch-all. That is by design — a table branching on a key must still deal to a character who lacks it. Note { "eq": null } is not expressible; absence and null alike just fail to match.

llm.description: Test the LLM consult's result — { "llm": { "eq": "YES" } } tests the answer; { "llm": { "ok": false } } tests whether the consult failed. Only valid on a tool that declares an `llm` block (enforced when the file loads, which JSON Schema cannot express here).

message.description: The narrative result, rendered verbatim. Supports {{value}} (final post-transform value), {{roll}} (raw pre-transform value), {{dice}} (dice breakdown, e.g. "3d6+2: [4, 2, 6] + 2 = 14"; empty for Form A), {{params.name}} (a resolved parameter), {{metadata.key}} (a key from the invoking character's fact sheet), {{state.path}} (a path into merged persistent state), and {{llm}} (the consult's output — the model's answer, or the llm block's errorMessage on failure). Unknown placeholders are left as written — as is a {{metadata.key}} the character lacks, a {{state.path}} that is absent or non-primitive, one holding a list or object, or {{llm}} on a tool with no llm block.

state.description: Semantic state of this outcome. Accents the result bubble; authors never write CSS.

NumberOperand.description: A number to compare against: a literal, a { "$param": "name" } reference to a declared numeric parameter, or a { "$state": "path", "fallback": <number> } reference. The reference form is how an opposed check is written — { "gte": { "$param": "difficulty" } } tests against a number the caller supplied rather than one fixed when the file was written.

AnyOperand.description: A value to compare against for eq/neq: a literal number, string, or boolean, a { "$param": "name" } reference to a declared parameter of any type, or a { "$state": "path", "fallback": ... } reference typed by its fallback.

StringOperand.description: The substring to look for with contains/ncontains: a non-empty literal string, a { "$param": "name" } reference to a declared string parameter, or a { "$state": "path", "fallback": ... } reference whose fallback is a string. Non-empty because every string contains "". That a referenced parameter or state fallback is a string is enforced when the file loads, which JSON Schema cannot express here.

NumericComparator.description: Comparisons against a number. Every key present must hold (they AND together).

ParamComparator.description: Comparisons against one declared parameter. Every key present must hold (they AND together). Identical to NumericComparator except that eq/neq widen to strings and booleans, since a parameter need not be a number.

gte.description: Greater than or equal to. Numeric parameters only.

lte.description: Less than or equal to. Numeric parameters only.

eq.description: Equal to. The operand's type must match the parameter's.

neq.description: Not equal to. The operand's type must match the parameter's.

contains.description: The parameter's text contains this substring (case-sensitive). String parameters only.

ncontains.description: The parameter's text does not contain this substring (case-sensitive). String parameters only.

MetadataComparator.description: Comparisons against one key of the invoking character's metadata sheet. Every key present must hold (they AND together). Shape-identical to ParamComparator; what differs is that nothing about the subject is knowable at load time, so every type rule below is enforced fail-soft at run time instead of as a rejection.

gt.description: Greater than. Does not match unless the key holds a number.

gte.description: Greater than or equal to. Does not match unless the key holds a number.

lt.description: Less than. Does not match unless the key holds a number.

lte.description: Less than or equal to. Does not match unless the key holds a number.

eq.description: Equal to. Does not match unless the key exists and holds a matching primitive.

neq.description: Not equal to. Does not match when the key is absent — absence is not inequality.

contains.description: Contains this substring (case-sensitive). Does not match unless the key holds a string.

ncontains.description: Does not contain this substring (case-sensitive). Does not match unless the key holds a string — as with neq, absence is not a miss.

LlmComparator.description: Comparisons against the LLM consult's result. The comparator keys test the answer; `ok` tests whether the consult succeeded at all. Every key present must hold (they AND together). Like metadata, the answer's run-time type is the model's business, so type rules are enforced fail-soft at run time: ordering comparators need the answer to parse as a number and otherwise decline the row; eq/neq compare numerically when both sides are numbers, and otherwise as trimmed, case-insensitive strings (trailing '.'/'!' forgiven) — an author who asked for "YES" should not lose to a model that said "yes.".

gt.description: Greater than. Does not match unless the answer parses as a number.

gte.description: Greater than or equal to. Does not match unless the answer parses as a number.

lt.description: Less than. Does not match unless the answer parses as a number.

lte.description: Less than or equal to. Does not match unless the answer parses as a number.

eq.description: Equal to, numerically or as trimmed case-insensitive text. On a failed consult the answer is the block's errorMessage.

neq.description: Not equal to, under the same reconciliation as eq.

contains.description: The answer contains this substring, compared trimmed and case-insensitively — 'west door' finds 'the West Door'.

ncontains.description: The answer does not contain this substring, under the same reconciliation as contains.

ok.description: true: this row only applies when the consult succeeded. false: only when it failed.

$param.description: Name of a declared parameter. One of the two forms of indirection in the format (the other is $state) — there are no expression strings anywhere.

StateRef.description: A reference into the merged persistent state (chat → project → group → general). The fallback is required and types the reference: it is what run-time resolution returns when the path is absent or holds a value of a different type, so a run can never fail on a $state reference.

$state.description: Dot/bracket path into merged state, e.g. "player.health" or "inventory[0].name".

fallback.description: Used whenever the path is absent or holds a value of a different type. In a roll field the fallback must be a number; a contains/ncontains operand's fallback must be a string. Enforced when the file loads.

NumberOrParamRef.description: A literal number, a reference to a declared parameter of type number or integer, or a { "$state": "path", "fallback": <number> } reference. That the referenced parameter is numeric, or the state fallback is a number, is enforced when the file loads, which JSON Schema cannot express here.
